mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S-Type 3.0 V6 (AJ30) engine typically costs £1,200–£3,500 supply only. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on workshop location. Used engines can be found from £800 upwards, with reconditioned engines offering warranty-backed options. Main dealer new engines are no longer available. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the AJ30 is generally a reliable and durable engine. It features an aluminium block and heads, chain-driven timing (no belt to worry about), and an estimated lifespan of around 370,000 km. However, it has known weak points: intake manifold gaskets can crack causing vacuum leaks, the variable intake geometry actuator can fail, and the PCV system requires regular cleaning to prevent oil leaks. Parts can be expensive.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6?

acceptedAnswer.text: Intake manifold gasket failure (vacuum leaks causing rough idle and hesitation), variable intake geometry actuator failure (loss of low-end torque), PCV valve clogging (oil leaks from seals), and timing chain tensioner wear (rattling on cold start). The engine is also known for expensive parts.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold: if your S-Type is worth £3,000–£6,000 and a recon engine is £2,500 (42–83%), it's borderline on lower-value examples. If the vehicle is in excellent condition, replacement may be justified. If it's a tired example, consider the overall condition carefully.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The S-Type 3.0 V6 uses the AJ30 (also known as the AJ-V6) — a 3.0-litre V6 producing 235–240 bhp. It was fitted to the X200 S-Type from 1999 to 2007. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the intake manifold gaskets, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The intake manifold gaskets can be repaired individually (£200–£500). This is a straightforward job that restores idle quality and performance. However, ignoring vacuum leaks can lead to the engine running lean, potentially causing damage to the catalytic converters or the engine itself. Address any vacuum leaks promptly.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the AJ30. See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. Reconditioned engines offer warranty-backed options. Some suppliers offer used S-Type 3.0 V6 engines at competitive prices.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S-Type 3.0 V6 engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The longitudinal V6 layout makes the engine relatively accessible compared to transverse installations.

cta.label: → Compare S-Type 3.0 V6 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Intake Manifold Gasket Failure (Vacuum Leaks)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, hissing sound from the engine bay. The engine may stall at idle.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–120,000 miles — gaskets can crack with age and heat cycling.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, the engine will run lean, potentially causing damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The intake manifold gaskets on the AJ30 can crack over time, causing vacuum leaks. This leads to rough idle, hesitation, and poor fuel economy. The repair is relatively straightforward, but if ignored, the engine can run lean, potentially causing damage to the catalytic converters or even the engine itself. If you notice any of these symptoms, have the intake manifold gaskets inspected promptly.

problems.title: Variable Intake Geometry Actuator Failure
problems.symptoms: Loss of low-end torque, reduced power, poor throttle response, engine warning light, increased fuel consumption.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — the electric actuator can fail with age.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, performance and fuel economy will suffer.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The AJ30 features an electric drive for changing the intake geometry, which is known for low reliability. This system optimises torque delivery across the rev range. When it fails, you'll notice a loss of low-end torque and reduced throttle response. Replacement is the only solution, and it's best to use OEM-quality parts. If you notice a decline in performance, have the intake system inspected.

problems.title: Crankcase Ventilation Valve (PCV) Clogging
problems.symptoms: Oil leaks from various seals and gaskets, increased oil consumption, rough idle, oil in the intake tract, engine smoking.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — the valve clogs with sludge over time.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil leaks will worsen and can lead to engine damage.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The crankcase ventilation valve on the AJ30 requires regular cleaning. If it becomes clogged, pressure builds in the crankcase, causing oil to be pushed out from various seals and gaskets. Regular maintenance of the PCV system is essential for preventing oil leaks. If you notice oil leaks or increased oil consumption, have the PCV system inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: S-Type 3.0 V6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple fix — restore idle and performance
Essential for full performance — replace when symptoms appear
Less common but can occur — repair if rattling
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S-Type 3.0 V6? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* No vacuum leaks or rough idle — intake gaskets are a known weak point
* No oil leaks from seals or gaskets
avoidIf: * Rough idle or hesitation — intake manifold gasket or intake actuator issues
* Oil leaks from multiple locations — PCV system may be clogged
* Rattling from the engine — timing chain tensioner wear is possible
* Poor service history — parts are expensive and neglect is costly

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ30 is a generally reliable and durable engine, but it's now an ageing unit with known weak points. Intake manifold gaskets, the variable intake actuator, and the PCV system are the main areas of concern. A £2,500 reconditioned engine on an S-Type valued at £3,000–£6,000 is 42–83% of the car's value — borderline on lower-value examples. With the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold, assess the overall condition of the vehicle carefully before committing to a replacement.

cta.label: → Compare S-Type 3.0 V6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar S-Type 3.0 V6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain tensioner wear)
* Idle Quality — Any rough idle or hesitation? (intake manifold gasket or actuator issues)
* Service History — Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and other seals (PCV system may be clogged)
* Vacuum Leaks — Listen for hissing sounds from the engine bay
* Performance — Test acceleration for smooth power delivery (intake actuator)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
